items.prompt_md: Why does topological sorting only make sense on a **directed** graph, not an undirected one?
options: Because the edges represent a one-way "must come before" relationship, and undirected edges have no such direction to order by
Because undirected graphs never contain cycles
Because DFS cannot traverse undirected graphs
Because undirected graphs always have more than one valid ordering
items.answer: Because the edges represent a one-way "must come before" relationship, and undirected edges have no such direction to order by
items.explanation_md: "A must happen before B" is inherently directional — an undirected edge only says two things are related, not which one comes first, so there's nothing for a topological order to respect.

items.prompt_md: What does it mean, in terms of the dependency graph, when a valid topological order does NOT exist?
options: The directed graph contains a cycle — some set of nodes each (indirectly) depend on one another
The graph is disconnected
The graph has more edges than nodes
Every node has an in-degree of zero
items.answer: The directed graph contains a cycle — some set of nodes each (indirectly) depend on one another
items.explanation_md: A cycle means node A depends (directly or transitively) on node B, which depends back on A — there's no way to place either one first, so no valid order can exist.

items.prompt_md: In the three-state DFS cycle check (unvisited / in-progress / done), why can't a plain two-state visited/unvisited set do the same job?
options: A two-state set can't distinguish "this node is an ancestor on the current path" (a cycle) from "this node was already fully explored via a different path" (a harmless diamond shape)
A two-state set uses more memory than three states
A two-state set cannot represent a directed graph at all
A two-state set always reports every graph as cyclic
items.answer: A two-state set can't distinguish "this node is an ancestor on the current path" (a cycle) from "this node was already fully explored via a different path" (a harmless diamond shape)
items.explanation_md: Re-reaching an already-fully-explored node is fine (two paths converging), but re-reaching a node still open on the current call stack is a cycle — only a third state ("in progress") tells those two situations apart.

items.prompt_md: In Kahn's algorithm (the BFS-based topological sort), what does a node's "in-degree" reaching zero signal?
options: Every one of that node's prerequisites has already been processed, so the node itself is now safe to process
The node has no outgoing edges to any other node
The node is part of a cycle
The node has been visited twice
items.answer: Every one of that node's prerequisites has already been processed, so the node itself is now safe to process
items.explanation_md: In-degree counts remaining unprocessed prerequisites; hitting zero means there's nothing left blocking that node, so it can be dequeued and processed next.

items.prompt_md: How does Kahn's algorithm detect a cycle without ever doing a DFS-style recursion?
options: If, after the queue empties, fewer nodes were processed than the total node count, the unprocessed remainder must all be stuck in a cycle
It detects a cycle the moment any node's in-degree exceeds the number of edges
It cannot detect cycles — only DFS can
It detects a cycle if the queue is ever empty at the very start
items.answer: If, after the queue empties, fewer nodes were processed than the total node count, the unprocessed remainder must all be stuck in a cycle
items.explanation_md: Nodes only leave the queue once their prerequisites are cleared; if a group of nodes all wait on each other in a cycle, their in-degree never drops to zero, so they're simply never dequeued — leaving a processed count short of the total.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of a topological sort (either DFS-based or Kahn's) on a graph with V nodes and E directed edges?
options: O(V + E)
O(V × E)
O(E log V)
items.answer: O(V + E)
items.explanation_md: Every node is processed once and every edge is examined once (to decrement an in-degree or to recurse across it), giving linear time in the size of the graph.

items.prompt_md: Building Kahn's algorithm, you forget to initialize the in-degree of nodes with zero incoming edges (leaving them absent from the in-degree map rather than set to 0). What breaks?
options: Those nodes never get enqueued at the start, so the algorithm can under-count processed nodes and wrongly report a cycle
The algorithm still works correctly, just slightly slower
It causes an infinite loop that never terminates
It only affects nodes with outgoing edges, not incoming ones
items.answer: Those nodes never get enqueued at the start, so the algorithm can under-count processed nodes and wrongly report a cycle
items.explanation_md: Kahn's algorithm seeds its queue with every node whose in-degree is exactly zero — if a node's in-degree was never recorded as 0 in the first place, it's silently skipped, which throws off both the initial queue and the final processed-count check.

items.prompt_md: In the DFS-based topological sort, in what order are nodes actually appended to the result before the final reversal?
options: Post-order — a node is appended only after every one of its descendants has already finished
Pre-order — a node is appended the instant DFS first visits it
In the same order the outer loop iterates over the nodes
Randomly, since order doesn't matter before reversal
items.answer: Post-order — a node is appended only after every one of its descendants has already finished
items.explanation_md: A node can only be safely placed once everything it depends on (transitively) is already placed, and that's exactly the moment DFS finishes exploring all of its neighbors — so recording finish order and reversing it produces a valid dependency order.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of a topological sort on a graph with V nodes and E edges, counting the adjacency list plus traversal state (recursion stack or queue and in-degree array)?
options: O(V + E)
O(log V)
O(V × E)
items.answer: O(V + E)
items.explanation_md: The adjacency list itself takes O(V + E), and the extra bookkeeping (a state array, in-degree counts, or a queue) adds at most O(V) more, so total space stays linear in the graph's size.

items.prompt_md: Which phrasing in a problem is the strongest signal to reach for topological sort rather than plain DFS/BFS reachability?
options: "Item X must happen before item Y" — a fixed set of items with directed "must precede" dependency constraints, asking for a valid order or whether one exists
"Find the minimum number of steps to reach a target"
"Count how many separate connected regions exist"
"Find the two elements that sum to a target"
items.answer: "Item X must happen before item Y" — a fixed set of items with directed "must precede" dependency constraints, asking for a valid order or whether one exists
items.explanation_md: Directed "before/after" dependency constraints over a fixed set of items, with the question being "does an order exist / what is it," is topological sort's defining fingerprint — shortest-path and pure-connectivity phrasing point elsewhere.

items.prompt_md: You're given N courses and a list of prerequisite pairs (directed "must come before" edges) and must decide if it's possible to finish all of them. Which approach fits?
options: Topological sort of the dependency graph, detecting a cycle via in-progress/done states or in-degree tracking
BFS layer by layer, counting the shortest path between two courses
Union-Find to check whether all courses are in the same connected component
Sort courses by their number of prerequisites and take them in that order
items.answer: Topological sort of the dependency graph, detecting a cycle via in-progress/done states or in-degree tracking
items.explanation_md: "Directed prerequisite edges, can everything finish" is the topological-sort fingerprint — Union-Find tests undirected connectivity, not directed cycles, so it can't distinguish a valid chain from a cyclic deadlock.

items.prompt_md: A build system has targets that depend on other targets being built first. You need a valid order to build all targets, or to detect that no such order exists. Which approach fits?
options: Topological sort (Kahn's in-degree BFS or DFS post-order) over the target dependency graph
Multi-source BFS seeded from every target with zero dependencies
A max-heap ordered by number of dependents
Two pointers scanning the target list from both ends
items.answer: Topological sort (Kahn's in-degree BFS or DFS post-order) over the target dependency graph
items.explanation_md: "Targets must be built after their dependencies" is a directed-dependency ordering question — exactly what topological sort answers, and a cycle here means the build is impossible.

items.prompt_md: You have a list of tasks and a set of "task A must run after task B" constraints. You need to produce one valid execution order for all the tasks. Which approach fits?
options: Topological sort of the task dependency graph
DFS flood fill from each task treated as a grid cell
A monotonic stack of tasks ordered by duration
Binary search over the number of tasks
items.answer: Topological sort of the task dependency graph
items.explanation_md: "Must run after" constraints over a fixed set of tasks is the same directed-dependency shape as course prerequisites — topological sort produces a valid order, or reveals a cycle if none exists.

items.prompt_md: Given a list of words known to be sorted according to some unknown alien alphabet's letter order, you must derive a valid ordering of the alphabet's letters consistent with that list. Which approach fits?
options: Build a directed graph of "letter X comes before letter Y" constraints from adjacent words, then topologically sort the letters
BFS from the first letter of the first word to find shortest paths between letters
A hash map counting how often each letter appears
Two pointers comparing the first and last word
items.answer: Build a directed graph of "letter X comes before letter Y" constraints from adjacent words, then topologically sort the letters
items.explanation_md: Once the pairwise "comes before" constraints between letters are extracted, deriving one consistent global order from them is exactly topological sort — and an inconsistency in the constraints shows up as a cycle.

items.prompt_md: A package manager tracks which packages depend on which others, and must detect whether any circular dependency exists before attempting an install. Which approach fits?
options: Topological sort / cycle detection over the directed dependency graph
Multi-source BFS to compute install time for each package
A min-heap ordered by number of dependents
Fast/slow pointers over the dependency chain
items.answer: Topological sort / cycle detection over the directed dependency graph
items.explanation_md: Circular-dependency detection is precisely the cycle check baked into topological sort — a cycle in the directed dependency graph means installation can never be ordered validly.

items.prompt_md: You're given the head of a singly linked list and must determine whether it contains a cycle. Which approach fits?
options: Fast/slow pointers (Floyd's algorithm) walking the list at different speeds until they either meet or one reaches the end
Topological sort of the list nodes to detect a back edge
Multi-source BFS seeded from every node
A hash map counting how many times each node value appears
items.answer: Fast/slow pointers (Floyd's algorithm) walking the list at different speeds until they either meet or one reaches the end
items.explanation_md: This is a cycle question, but over a singly linked list's single-successor chain, not a general dependency graph — the fast/slow pointer technique is the linked-list-specific tool for it, not topological sort.

items.prompt_md: You must check whether a string of brackets like `{[()]}` is properly balanced and nested. Which approach fits?
options: A stack that pushes opening brackets and pops/matches on each closing bracket
Topological sort of the bracket characters
BFS layer by layer over the characters
Union-Find merging matching brackets
items.answer: A stack that pushes opening brackets and pops/matches on each closing bracket
items.explanation_md: Bracket matching is the classic stack pattern — there's no dependency graph or ordering question here, just nested pairs that resolve most-recent-first.

items.prompt_md: You have two already-sorted arrays and must merge them into one sorted array. Which approach fits?
options: Two pointers, one per array, advancing whichever points to the smaller current element
Topological sort of the combined elements
DFS flood fill over the two arrays
Multi-source BFS seeded from both array starts
items.answer: Two pointers, one per array, advancing whichever points to the smaller current element
items.explanation_md: Merging two sorted sequences is the standard two-pointer merge — there's no dependency ordering or graph structure to exploit here, just two indices advancing in step.

items.prompt_md: A recipe app has steps for a meal, some of which require other steps to be finished first (e.g., "boil water" before "cook pasta"). You must determine if all steps can be completed, and if so, in what order. Which approach fits?
options: Topological sort of the step dependency graph
BFS to find the shortest path from the first step to the last
A max-heap ordered by how many steps depend on each step
Backtracking over every possible step ordering
items.answer: Topological sort of the step dependency graph
items.explanation_md: "Some steps require other steps first" is a directed dependency constraint over a fixed set of items — topological sort both confirms an order exists and produces one, and a cycle in the requirements means it's unsolvable.

items.prompt_md: Given a directed graph of job dependencies within a company workflow, you must determine whether a valid execution order for all jobs exists, and report which is the case. Which approach fits?
options: Topological sort (Kahn's or DFS-based), reporting failure if a cycle is detected
DFS flood fill counting the number of reachable jobs
A monotonic stack of jobs ordered by deadline
Two pointers scanning the job list from both ends
items.answer: Topological sort (Kahn's or DFS-based), reporting failure if a cycle is detected
items.explanation_md: "Does a valid execution order exist" over directed job dependencies is topological sort's exact question — a cycle among the jobs is what makes the answer no.
